revised_stoic_quote: How beautifully Plato put it. Whenever you want to talk about people, it's best to take a bird's-eye view and see everything all at once: gatherings, armies, farms, weddings and divorces, births and deaths, noisy courtrooms or silent spaces, every foreign people, holidays, memorials, markets, all blended together and arranged in a pairing of opposites.
revised_stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus invite a way of seeing that finds splendor and coherence in the whole, not by ignoring particulars but by recognizing them as expressions of something unified.

revised_stoic_quote: The first rule is to keep an untroubled spirit. The second is to look things in the face and know them for what they are.
revised_stoic_bridge: Both the Gita's guna framework and Marcus's instruction to 'know things for what they are' provide diagnostic tools for your inner states: name the pattern and you gain power over it.
verification_note: Meditations 5.26 is in Casaubon Book 5. This wording may be from a modern translation. Casaubon equivalent to be confirmed.
notes: BG 14.5 is about recognizing which guna is operating in you at any moment. Marcus 5.26 is about looking at things clearly and knowing them for what they are. This is a natural diagnostic pairing. The 10.16 quote ('be a good person') had nothing to do with the three gunas.

verification_note: Meditations 3.11 is in Casaubon Book 3 (likely section IX or X). Casaubon wording differs. Passage is genuine Marcus on seeing things clearly.
notes: BG 15.10 distinguishes those with the 'eye of knowledge' from the deluded who don't see. Marcus 3.11 is specifically about training yourself to see things as they actually are, stripped of opinion. This is a much more precise match than 6.52 (about choosing not to be harmed), which belongs with verse 41's environmental immunity theme.

revised_stoic_quote: A good man will do what he thinks it will be honourable for him to do, even if it involves toil; he will do it even if it involves harm to him; he will do it even if it involves peril; again, he will not do that which will be base, even if it brings him money, or pleasure, or power.
revised_stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Seneca describe the same standard for excellent action: do what is right regardless of cost or reward, and refuse what is base regardless of what it offers.
verification_note: Found in seneca-letters-public-domain.txt, Letter LXXVI.
notes: Wait: 76.18 is already used for verse 48 (BG 6.40). However, BG 6.40 is about effort never being wasted, while BG 18.23 is about the criteria for pure action. If the same Seneca passage can serve both themes, this duplication is less harmful than reusing Epictetus 4.1.62-64 verbatim. Alternative: use Seneca, Moral Letters 71.16 ('The wise man does nothing which he could regret, nothing against his will, but does everything nobly, consistently, seriously, and rightly') for verse 88 instead. This needs verification but is a better unique match.
